pair.title: Show HN: MDV – a Markdown superset for docs, dashboards, and slides with data

story.title: GitHub - drasimwagan/mdv: MDV — a Markdown superset for documents, dashboards, and slides with embedded data and visualizations. HTML + PDF export, live preview, VS Code extension.

comments.text: This is exactly what I've been wanting. The idea of writing dashboards and reports in a single Markdown workflow instead of switching between three different tools is compelling. The constraint of no selectors or expressions actually seems like a strength here – it forces clarity.

kids.text: I spun up the example and the stat block rendered fine, but the line chart with `series=region` didn't split into separate lines. The CSV has region as a column, should that work out of the box or do I need a different syntax? Checked the docs quickly and didn't find it spelled

This is basically Markdown Report Generator v47. I built something almost identical in 2019 with Pandoc and custom filters, and it solved exactly zero of the actual problems. The real issue isn't the authoring format-it's that your data pipeline is disconnected from your presentation layer. Six months in, you're maintaining the CSV manually anyway, the charts drift, and nobody trusts the numbers. Markdown doesn't fix that. A proper BI tool with live connections does, but then you don't get the pretty prose layer. There's a reason people use three tools.

comments.text:
I've been pushing for something like this at work for the past year. Our workflow is painful: analysts write SQL queries and dump CSVs, designers mock up dashboards in Figma, then someone (usually me) rebuilds the whole thing in our BI tool, and execs want it as slides for board meetings. Three separate tools, three separate sources of truth, three opportunities for the data to drift.

The constraint here is actually the feature. No code, no expressions, no selectors. That's the opposite of what you'd expect from a "superset," but it's exactly right. Most reports don't need Turing completeness; they need consistency and speed. Named styles + YAML datasets means a junior analyst can produce something that looks professional without learning a templating language.

Two questions from someone who'd actually adopt this: how does it handle incremental

kids.text: This reminds me of the mid-2000s when we tried to solve the same problem with custom XML tooling and XSLT transforms. Looked good on paper, but the moment someone wanted a bar chart instead of a line chart, you were back in the weeds tuning stylesheets. The "no code" constraint

This reads like the dashboard tools we abandoned ten years ago. Jaspersoft, BIRT, Crystal Reports, all promising the unified workflow. They all ended up as nightmares to maintain because the moment you need something slightly custom, you're either stuck or writing code anyway. The "no code" constraint

> The "no code" constraint

I'd push back on the Jaspersoft comparison. Those tools failed because they tried to be everything to everyone and ended up with 400 configuration options. MDV looks intentionally narrower-it's "Markdown for reports," not "replace your entire BI

comments.text: Stop thinking of this as a reporting tool and start thinking of it as a lingua franca for data work. The real insight isn't "write once, export three ways" but rather "stop forcing data analysts, designers, and executives to speak different dialect of the same story." Git-diffable reports, version control on dashboards, code review for KPI changes, reproducible slides. That's the actual game shift here.

kids.text:
> stop forcing data analysts, designers, and executives to speak different dialect of the same story

"Lingua franca" is the wrong metaphor here. A lingua franca is a neutral bridge language. This is asking three groups to all switch to Markdown, which is only neutral if you think plain text is

This reads like a stealth pitch for Observable notebooks or Jupyter, but with stricter syntax. The real precedent isn't "write once, export three ways", it's literate programming (Knuth) meeting reproducible research (Rmarkdown). Git-diffable is the constraint that wins

> literate programming (Knuth) meeting reproducible research (Rmarkdown)

This is just Rmarkdown with different branding and stricter constraints. Knuth's literate programming required *code execution* embedded in the narrative. MDV strips that out entirely. You can't param

comments.text: How does this handle the "export to PDF" claim? I've seen a lot of markdown-to-PDF tools that work great on simple docs but fall apart with charts and tables. Does it use headless Chrome under the hood, or is it rendering to something more portable? The examples on the repo all look

kids.text: PDFs are rendered to SVG first, then the SVG is embedded in the PDF. This avoids the headless browser overhead entirely. For charts it works well, but table layout can be fragile if you have lots of columns or very long cell content. The HTML export is where it really shines since

I built something similar for internal reporting at a fintech startup about three years ago, and we ended up going the SVG-embed route too after trying wkhtmltopdf first. The fragility you mention with table columns is real, we had to add manual column-width hints in YAML, which

comments.text: Stop thinking of this as a tool replacement and start thinking of it as a unification layer. The real win isn't "markdown for dashboards", it's collapsing three separate workflows (doc, BI, deck) into one source of truth. You write once, export everywhere. That's the reframe that matters. Most tools try to be all three and fail at being one. This deliberately chooses to be excellent at the single input format and lets the output targets handle themselves.

kids.text: I work with a lot of reporting tools and the unification angle is real, but CommonMark strict is going to be a hard sell for teams already in Jupyter or Observable notebooks. Those let you inline computation. MDV seems to require pre-computed CSVs or static data blocks. That's a constraint
